titleStmt.title: Letter from Deborah Webb to Hanna Sheehy Skeffington, 14 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Deborah Webb

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from Deborah Webb to Hanna Sheehy Skeffington about her recent letter from Sheehy-Skeffington and to discuss suffragette matters.
Deborah Webb was a member of the Irish Women's Franchise League and a nationalist.
Hanna Sheehy Skeffington (1877-1946) suffragette, nationalist, language teacher, was the founder of the Irish Women’s Franchise League and a founding member of the Irish Women Workers’ Union. She was the widow of Francis Sheehy-Skeffington who was summarily executed on 26 April 1916. She was active during the Rising, bringing food to the Volunteers in the G.P.O. and the College of Surgeons. Four days passed before she found out what had happened to her husband, Francis (1878-1916), and it wasn't until almost two weeks later that the full details of his execution emerged.

msIdentifier.collection: Sheehy-Skeffington Papers, Collection List No. 47; ms 33,604/17-20

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
9 Garville Road March 14th 1916 Dear Hanna It was good to hear from
you. I am sending your letter
on to J.C. She had told me
about your sister & her
beautiful children & I know
she will be sorry for what
you tell. Your troubles & joys
are always more or less
troubles & joys to me too.
I can sympathize with you
about losing your Mary &
do hope she will have a
worthy succession. That was such a serious
operation that although
successful it will take a
long time to recover from. I can now creep into the
sitting room but do not
yet with my own letters.
You will like to know that
a very kind & congenial
friend is homing with me
which very much adds to
my happiness. The doctors say that I shall
gradually gain ground. Be
sure that when able I shall
much enjoy seeing you
but that will not be for
a while & we understand
each other. I look forward
to the I.C. tomorrow Yours affectionately D.W.
for C.W.H. My good doctor J.C. Purser
says he is not only a
suffragist but a suffragette, as
he sympathizes on that point
a on many others.
marriage dangerous trade. Economic problem at root.
Women face a proposition to economic
independence.
Risks excluded.
Marriage marked C. Herrulto
Prostitution
Intensified by war.
Polygamy & independence. How to be achieved 1) Woman continues her work
No longer trained to marry. 2) If she specializes let her
be paid Objections:
Sentiment - Mar. Wom's Prop.
Difficulty of diviser Mrs. Gilma
To her a German said.
unreputable.
unoccupied. Giving a present
office.
